request.question: Dissident Irish Republican campaign includes which paramilitary group that began attacks around 2009?

title: Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group)
Irish security forces
Dissident Irish Republican campaign
Irish National Liberation Army
United Ireland
Timeline of Continuity IRA actions
Real Irish Republican Army
Timeline of Real Irish Republican Army actions
Provisional Irish Republican Army
Official Irish Republican Army
sentences: Óglaigh na hÉireann (ONH; ] ) is the title taken by a small dissident Irish republican paramilitary group that has taken part in the dissident Irish republican campaign.
The organisation started carrying out attacks around 2009 and was formed after a split within the Real IRA.
Irish security forces is a term used to describe all security forces of the Republic of Ireland.

The term was widely used to refer to the various security operations undertaken by the Irish state during The Troubles, and still continues to be used to describe security operations aimed at undermining the ongoing dissident Irish Republican campaign.

Since the Provisional Irish Republican Army (IRA) called a ceasefire and ended its armed campaign in 1997, breakaway groups opposed to the ceasefire ("dissident Irish republicans") have continued a low-level armed campaign against the British security forces in Northern Ireland.
The main paramilitaries involved are the Real IRA, Continuity IRA and Óglaigh na hÉireann.
They have targeted the British Army and Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI, successor of the Royal Ulster Constabulary) in gun and bomb attacks, as well as with mortars and rockets.
They have also carried out bombings that are meant to cause disruption.
However, their campaign has not been as intensive as the Provisional IRA's.
The Irish National Liberation Army (INLA, Irish: "Arm Saoirse Náisiúnta na hÉireann" ) is an Irish republican socialist paramilitary group formed in December 1974, during "the Troubles".
It seeks to remove Northern Ireland from the United Kingdom and create a socialist republic encompassing all of Ireland.
It is the paramilitary wing of the Irish Republican Socialist Party (IRSP).
United Ireland is the proposition that the whole of Ireland should be a single sovereign state.
At present, the island is divided politically; the sovereign state of Ireland has jurisdiction over the majority of Ireland, while Northern Ireland is part of the United Kingdom.
Achieving a united Ireland is a central tenet of Irish nationalism, particularly of both mainstream and dissident Irish republican political and paramilitary organisations.
Unionists support Northern Ireland remaining part of the United Kingdom, and therefore oppose Irish unification.
This is a chronology of activities by the Continuity Irish Republican Army (CIRA), an Irish republican paramilitary group.
The group started operations in 1994, after the Provisional Irish Republican Army began a ceasefire.
The Real Irish Republican Army or Real IRA (RIRA), is a dissident Irish republican paramilitary organisation which aims to bring about a united Ireland.
It formed in 1997 following a split in the Provisional IRA by dissident members, who rejected the IRA's ceasefire that year.

Like the Provisional IRA before it, the RIRA sees itself as the only rightful successor to the original Irish Republican Army and styles itself as "the Real Irish Republican Army" in English or "Óglaigh na hÉireann" in Irish.
It is an illegal organisation in the Republic of Ireland and designated as proscribed terrorist organisation in the United Kingdom and the United States.
This is a timeline of actions by the Real Irish Republican Army, also called the Real IRA, an Irish republican paramilitary group.
The group was formed in late 1997 by members of the Provisional Irish Republican Army who disagreed with that organisation's ceasefire.

The Provisional Irish Republican Army (Provisional IRA) was an Irish republican paramilitary organization that sought to remove Northern Ireland from the United Kingdom and to bring about an independent socialist republic encompassing all of Ireland.
It was the biggest and most active republican paramilitary group during the Troubles.
It saw itself as the successor to the original IRA and called itself simply the Irish Republican Army (IRA), or "Óglaigh na hÉireann" in Irish, and was widely referred to as such by others.

The Official Irish Republican Army or Official IRA (OIRA) was an Irish republican paramilitary group whose goal was to remove Northern Ireland from the United Kingdom and create a "workers' republic" encompassing all of Ireland.
It emerged in December 1969, shortly after the beginning of the Troubles, when the Irish Republican Army split into two factions.
The other was the Provisional IRA.
Each continued to call itself simply "the IRA" and rejected the other's legitimacy.
Unlike the "Provisionals", the "Officials" were Marxist and worked to form a united front with other Irish communist groups, named the Irish National Liberation Front (NLF).
The Officials were called the NLF by the Provisionals and were sometimes nicknamed the "Red IRA" by others.

title: Dissident Irish Republican campaign
Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group)
Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group)

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- If summary_1 already includes explicit profession/role/descriptor tokens for an anchor (for example, 'singer', 'director', 'country music artist'), omit those redundant descriptor tokens and emit a compact anchor-only query consisting of the core visible anchors (names, titles, groups). Retain relation tokens only when the question explicitly requests them or when disambiguation rules demand them. Do not remove descriptors when the question explicitly asks for role/occupation evidence or when a short-title disambiguation rule requires adding an entertainment disambiguator.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity (or otherwise names candidate/bridge entities), treat the context claim as the summary anchor and perform explicit identity-alignment checks before using passage facts. For each entity explicitly named in `context` as a candidate or bridge, do the following: if at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append a single brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that linking fact and references the corroborating passage and also include one short clause for that context-named entity giving the passage-supported relation value (e.g., "Name: [relation value]."); if no passage provides the relation value for that context-named entity, append a short clause stating that no relation value is provided in the evidence (e.g., "Name: no [relation] provided in evidence."). Do not create such clauses for entities that are only mentioned in `passages` and are not named in `context`. Do not append corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep these additions short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.
- Vocal-role single-token normalization (narrow scope): If summary_2 (alone or composed with summary_1 where identity links are explicit) explicitly contains vocal-role evidence — i.e., one or more verbalized vocal-role phrases that clearly denote a singing performer (examples: "lead singer", "lead vocals", "vocalist" as presented in the summaries) — then you may prefer to emit the concise single-token common noun for a person who sings (e.g., "singer") only when that single-token common noun itself appears verbatim in the summaries or when the vocal-role phrases in the summaries unambiguously denote the same person-role. If those explicit lexical licenses are not present, retain the literal role phrasing as given in summary_2 and do not normalize modifiers or drop qualifiers.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Dissident Irish Republican campaign includes which paramilitary group that began attacks around 2009?
hop1_docs: Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group) | Óglaigh na hÉireann (ONH; ] ) is the title taken by a small dissident Irish republican paramilitary group that has taken part in the dissident Irish republican campaign.  The organisation started carrying out attacks around 2009 and was formed after a split within the Real IRA.

Dissident Irish Republican campaign | Since the Provisional Irish Republican Army (IRA) called a ceasefire and ended its armed campaign in 1997, breakaway groups opposed to the ceasefire ("dissident Irish republicans") have continued a low-level armed campaign against the British security forces in Northern Ireland.  The main paramilitaries involved are the Real IRA, Continuity IRA and Óglaigh na hÉireann.  They have targeted the British Army and Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI, successor of the Royal Ulster Constabulary) in gun and bomb attacks, as well as with mortars and rockets.  They have also carried out bombings that are meant to cause disruption.  However, their campaign has not been as intensive as the Provisional IRA's.

Dissident republican | Dissident republicans, renegade republicans, anti-Agreement republicans or anti-ceasefire republicans (Irish: "poblachtach easaontach" ) are Irish republicans who do not support the current peace agreements in Northern Ireland.  The agreements followed a 30-year conflict known as the Troubles, which claimed over 3,500 lives.  During the conflict, republican paramilitary groups such as the Provisional Irish Republican Army waged a campaign to bring about a united Irish republic.  Peace negotiations in the 1990s led to an IRA ceasefire in 1994 and to the Good Friday Agreement of 1998.  Mainstream republicans, represented by Sinn Féin, supported the Agreement as a means of achieving Irish unity peacefully.  'Dissidents' saw this as an abandonment of republican ideals and acceptance of partition and British rule.  They hold that the Northern Ireland Assembly and Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) are illegitimate and see the PSNI as a "British paramilitary police force".

Real Irish Republican Army | The Real Irish Republican Army or Real IRA (RIRA), is a dissident Irish republican paramilitary organisation which aims to bring about a united Ireland.  It formed in 1997 following a split in the Provisional IRA by dissident members, who rejected the IRA's ceasefire that year.  Like the Provisional IRA before it, the RIRA sees itself as the only rightful successor to the original Irish Republican Army and styles itself as "the Real Irish Republican Army" in English or "Óglaigh na hÉireann" in Irish.  It is an illegal organisation in the Republic of Ireland and designated as proscribed terrorist organisation in the United Kingdom and the United States.

Timeline of Continuity IRA actions | This is a chronology of activities by the Continuity Irish Republican Army (CIRA), an Irish republican paramilitary group.  The group started operations in 1994, after the Provisional Irish Republican Army began a ceasefire.

Irish Republican Voice | Irish Republican Voice was a political group founded by dissident republicans.  The group was believed to be the political wing of the group Saoirse na hEireann (itself an RIRA splinter group).  The IRV have denied this though they say they recognise it as the only legitimate army.  Members include prominent dissident republicans such as Paul Stewart and James McDonagh.

Fenian dynamite campaign | The Fenian dynamite campaign (or Fenian bombing campaign) was a paramilitary campaign orchestrated by Irish republicans against the British Empire, between the years 1881 and 1885.  The campaign was associated with Fenianism; that is to say the Irish revolutionary organisations which aimed to establish an independent Irish Republic; such as the Irish Republican Brotherhood, the Fenian Brotherhood, Clan na Gael and the United Irishmen of America.  The campaign, led by Jeremiah O'Donovan Rossa and other Irishmen exiled in the United States, was a form of asymmetrical warfare and targeted infrastructure, government, military and police targets in Great Britain (particularly London).  The campaign led to the establishment of secret police group Special Branch (originally known as the Special Irish Branch).
hop1_titles: Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group)
response.summary_1: Passages identify Óglaigh na hÉireann (ONH) as a dissident Irish republican paramilitary involved in the dissident campaign: e.g., passage [1] states "'Óglaigh na hÉireann ... is the title taken by a small dissident Irish republican paramilitary group that has taken part in the dissident Irish republican campaign,'" and "'The organisation started carrying out attacks around January 1, 2009 and was formed after a split within the Real IRA.'" Passage [2] also lists ONH among "the Real IRA, Continuity IRA and Óglaigh na hÉireann" as the main paramilitaries. Bridge entity: Óglaigh na hÉireann (ONH). The passages supply the needed identification and the timing claim; no additional identity link is required for the next hop.
response.hop2_query: Óglaigh na hÉireann ONH 2009 attacks dissident campaign
hop2_docs: Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group) | Óglaigh na hÉireann (ONH; ] ) is the title taken by a small dissident Irish republican paramilitary group that has taken part in the dissident Irish republican campaign.  The organisation started carrying out attacks around 2009 and was formed after a split within the Real IRA.

Dissident Irish Republican campaign | Since the Provisional Irish Republican Army (IRA) called a ceasefire and ended its armed campaign in 1997, breakaway groups opposed to the ceasefire ("dissident Irish republicans") have continued a low-level armed campaign against the British security forces in Northern Ireland.  The main paramilitaries involved are the Real IRA, Continuity IRA and Óglaigh na hÉireann.  They have targeted the British Army and Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI, successor of the Royal Ulster Constabulary) in gun and bomb attacks, as well as with mortars and rockets.  They have also carried out bombings that are meant to cause disruption.  However, their campaign has not been as intensive as the Provisional IRA's.

Óglaigh na hÉireann | Óglaigh na hÉireann (] ), abbreviated ÓÉ, is an Irish-language idiom that can be translated variously as "soldiers of Ireland", "warriors of Ireland", "volunteers of Ireland" or "Irish volunteers".  In traditional Gaelic script, it is written Óglaıġ na hÉıreann.

Real Irish Republican Army | The Real Irish Republican Army or Real IRA (RIRA), is a dissident Irish republican paramilitary organisation which aims to bring about a united Ireland.  It formed in 1997 following a split in the Provisional IRA by dissident members, who rejected the IRA's ceasefire that year.  Like the Provisional IRA before it, the RIRA sees itself as the only rightful successor to the original Irish Republican Army and styles itself as "the Real Irish Republican Army" in English or "Óglaigh na hÉireann" in Irish.  It is an illegal organisation in the Republic of Ireland and designated as proscribed terrorist organisation in the United Kingdom and the United States.

Organisation of National Ex-Servicemen | The Organisation of National Ex-Servicemen and Women (Irish: "Óglaigh Náisiúnta Na hÉireann" ) is a support organisation for ex-service personnel of the Irish Defence Services.

Defence Forces (Ireland) | The military of Ireland, known as the Defence Forces (Irish: "Fórsaí Cosanta" , officially styled "Óglaigh na hÉireann" ), encompass the Army, Air Corps, Naval Service and Reserve Defence Forces.

Irish Republican Army (1922–69) | The original Irish Republican Army (IRA) fought a guerrilla war against British rule in Ireland in the Irish War of Independence between 1919 and 1921.  Following the signing of the Anglo-Irish Treaty on 6 December 1921, the IRA in the 26 counties that were to become the Irish Free State split between supporters and opponents of the Treaty.  The anti-Treatyites, sometimes referred to by Free State forces as Irregulars, continued to use the name Irish Republican Army (IRA) or in Irish Óglaigh na hÉireann, as did the organisation in Northern Ireland which originally supported the pro-Treaty side (if not the Treaty). " Óglaigh na hÉireann" was also adopted as the name of the pro-Treaty National Army, and remains the official legal title of the Irish Defence Forces.  This article deals with the anti-Treaty IRA that fought against the Irish Free State in the Irish Civil War, and with its successors up to 1969, when the IRA split again.
hop2_titles: Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group)
response.summary_2: The dissident Irish Republican campaign includes the paramilitary Óglaigh na hÉireann (ONH). Passage [1] corroborates that ONH began carrying out attacks around 2009 and was formed after a split from the Real IRA; passage [2] also lists ONH among the main dissident paramilitaries.
